summary: Jeff Atwood enthusiastically recommends A.K. Dewdney's 'The New Turing Omnibus,' a 1988 book covering 66 computer science topics in accessible, bite-sized chapters. He draws a direct parallel between the book's format and his own blog—both offer grab-bag explorations of computing topics that readers can browse selectively. The post emphasizes the importance of practicing fundamentals, citing examples from go players, athletes, and musicians who continuously revisit basics. Atwood positions the book as valuable for students, professors, and educated laypeople alike, while raising the question of what fundamentals programmers should practice.
key_points: The New Turing Omnibus covers 66 computer science topics in short, accessible chapters with diagrams and illustrations
The book's format mirrors blog-style writing—browse what interests you, skip the rest
Even experts benefit from repeatedly practicing fundamentals, as illustrated by a high-level go player reading beginner books
The book serves multiple audiences: students previewing concepts, professors reviewing material, and curious laypeople
Programmers lack a clear consensus on what 'practicing fundamentals' means for their craft
money_quotes: The New Turing Omnibus is probably the single closest published equivalent to what I do on this very blog.
Browse and find something you like; discard the rest.
My goal each time is to see how much more quickly I can solve all the problems in the book than I did the last time.
Some of the best athletes never forget the fundamentals – whether it's Tiger Woods practicing the basics, or a pro basketball player working on free throws.
What would be the fundamentals that a good programmer might forget?
key_insight: Even expert programmers should continuously revisit and practice computer science fundamentals, though the profession lacks a clear consensus on what those fundamentals are.
